title: Add real iOS shell and APNs setup so native push works on iPhone
description:
Problem
A tracker who opens the app on an iPhone in Chrome sees the Native Push card and can tap Enable Native Push, but the app responds that native push is only available inside the Android or iPhone app shell. This is currently true behavior, but it reveals a real product gap: iPhone native push cannot work yet because the repo does not have an iOS Capacitor shell checked in and the iOS native/APNs setup has not been completed.

Why this happens today

* The React app only enables native push when Capacitor reports a native platform runtime.
* Opening the site in Chrome or Safari on iPhone is still a browser session, not the native Capacitor shell.
* The repo currently includes Android native shell support, but iOS setup still depends on a Mac/Xcode workflow.
* The settings copy implies iPhone support exists if the app is opened on a phone, which is misleading until the real iOS shell exists.

Goal
Make native push actually work on iPhone by shipping a real iOS app shell and completing the APNs setup, while also tightening the settings copy so browser users are not misled.

Execution plan once we have a Mac

 1. Add the iOS Capacitor shell with `npx cap add ios` and confirm the shared web build syncs cleanly into the generated Xcode project.
 2. Open the iOS project in Xcode, set the app target bundle identifier, choose the correct Apple team, and verify signing succeeds on a physical iPhone.
 3. Enable the required capabilities in Xcode: Push Notifications plus Background Modes with Remote notifications.
 4. Create or confirm the Apple Developer portal resources: App ID, APNs auth key, key ID, and team ID, all aligned to the chosen bundle identifier.
 5. Configure Supabase secrets for APNs delivery: `APNS_KEY_ID`, `APNS_TEAM_ID`, `APNS_BUNDLE_ID`, `APNS_PRIVATE_KEY`, and `APNS_USE_SANDBOX=true` for development.
 6. Build and run the native iOS shell on a physical iPhone with `VITE_PUSH_ENVIRONMENT=sandbox` so development tokens are stored with the correct environment.
 7. Log in as a tracker inside the installed iPhone app, open Settings, and enable native push. Verify that permission prompts appear and registration succeeds.
 8. Confirm a row is written to `public.push_notification_devices` with `platform = 'ios'`, `enabled = true`, and `environment = 'sandbox'`.
 9. Send a support message from a connected outsider session and verify the tracker receives an APNs notification on iPhone.
10. Tap the notification and confirm the app opens to the intended support destination via the existing app URL handling.
11. Improve the Settings card copy so browser sessions clearly explain that native push requires the installed native app, not just opening the site on a phone browser.
12. Document the final local build, signing, APNs secret, and validation steps so future iPhone testing is repeatable.

Definition of done

* A real iOS Capacitor shell exists and runs on a physical iPhone.
* Native push registration succeeds from the installed iPhone app.
* Supabase stores iPhone device tokens with the correct environment.
* Support-message push notifications are delivered to iPhone and deep-link correctly into the app.
* Settings copy no longer suggests that mobile browser sessions can enable native push.

Validation notes

* Test on a real iPhone, not the simulator.
* Keep development testing on APNs sandbox until release prep.
* Before TestFlight/App Store distribution, switch APNs delivery to production and re-verify token environment handling.

title: Fix tracking nav dropping Appointments after switching from Log to Mood
description:
Bug report:\\n- Open the tracker and tap Log\\n- Tap Mood\\n- The Appointments button disappears from the tracker section switcher\\n\\nExpected:\\n- Appointments should remain visible in the section switcher when moving between Log sub-pages\\n\\nActual:\\n- Appointments disappears after navigating from Log to Mood\\n\\nNotes:\\n- This looks like a tracker navigation/state regression and can be investigated later.

Implementation plan

1. Trace the tracker navigation state between Log, Mood, and Appointments to find where Appointments disappears.
2. Refactor the section-switcher visibility rules so enabled tracker areas drive the nav instead of the last visited sub-page.
3. Verify the fix across desktop, mobile, refresh, and back-navigation flows.
4. Add a regression test or QA checklist so this navigation bug does not come back.

Definition of done

* Appointments stays visible while moving between Log sub-pages.
* Tracker navigation remains consistent across mobile and desktop.
* The regression is covered by a repeatable check.

Progress update (2026-04-02)

* Fixed locally in src/pages/tracker/MoodPage.jsx by including Appointments in the Mood page section switcher list.
* Verified with npm run build.

title: Expand outsider overview with todo, appointments, and period summaries
description:
Update the outsider Overview page to surface summary-level blocks for todos, appointments, and period context without turning the page into tracker parity. Empty states should stay clear when only some categories are shared.

Implementation plan

1. Define the exact outcome for expand outsider overview with todo, appointments, and period summaries in the outsider experience without drifting into tracker parity.
2. Implement the necessary data, UI, and permission-safe rendering changes on top of the shared outsider summary model.

* Privacy boundaries still hold across mixed sharing states.
* Desktop and mobile outsider flows remain usable.

title: Add overdue, skipped, and carry-forward task handling
description:
Teach To-Do items how to age across days so the app can distinguish overdue work, intentionally skipped work, and tasks that should carry forward into the next day.

Implementation plan

1. Define the smallest data and UX changes needed for add overdue, skipped, and carry-forward task handling without making To-Do feel heavy.
2. Implement the required task model, form, and list updates while preserving existing task behavior.
3. Propagate the new task state through overview and planning summaries where it adds value.
4. Verify backward compatibility, cross-day behavior, and task edge cases before closing the issue.

Definition of done

* The targeted To-Do improvement works in storage and UI.
* Existing task data continues to behave correctly.
* The change supports future task work instead of adding coupling.

Bitwarden's browser autofill overlay throws a runtime error on our auth screen:

`bootstrap-autofill-overlay.js:9562 Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'includes')`

This still happens after we updated the login/signup/password-reset fields to use more standard form semantics (`form` submit handling, `name`, and `autocomplete` hints).

## Impact

* Users with the Bitwarden autofill extension see a console error on our auth experience.
* The page appears usable, but this is noisy and may indicate Bitwarden is failing to classify some fields correctly.
* We should aim for our auth UI to behave cleanly with Bitwarden because it is a common password manager.

## Error

`bootstrap-autofill-overlay.js:9562 Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'includes')`

Stack excerpt:

* `AutofillOverlayContentService.setQualifiedLoginFillType`
* `AutofillOverlayContentService.isIgnoredField`

## Current app-side mitigation already tried

* Added `onSubmit` form semantics for login.
* Added explicit field names and autocomplete hints:
  * login email: `autocomplete=username`
  * login password: `autocomplete=current-password`
  * signup password fields: `autocomplete=new-password`
  * reset-password verification field: `autocomplete=current-password`
  * PIN-only fields: `autocomplete=off`

Files touched:

* `src/App.jsx`
* `src/pages/tracker/SettingsPage.jsx`

## Investigation notes

* The stack trace points at Bitwarden-injected code, not our bundled app code.
* There are recent public reports that match this same Bitwarden overlay error, so this may be an upstream extension bug.
* We should still document any DOM patterns in our app that make Bitwarden more likely to mis-detect fields.

## Acceptance ideas

* Reproduce with Bitwarden enabled on the auth screen.
* Confirm whether any remaining field markup can be made more Bitwarden-friendly.
* If no additional local workaround exists, track upstream and document as external compatibility issue.

## Implementation plan

1. Reproduce consistently with Bitwarden enabled on the login, signup, and PIN reset flows in desktop Chrome and one Chromium-based mobile wrapper build if available.
2. Capture a minimal DOM snapshot of the auth fields at the moment Bitwarden injects its overlay so we can compare field names, ids, labels, autocomplete values, and form ancestry.
3. Normalize auth markup further if needed:
   * add stable input ids paired with explicit `label htmlFor` or equivalent accessible relationships
   * ensure each auth flow is wrapped in a distinct `form`
   * avoid ambiguous password-like fields in the same visual section when possible
   * keep non-account PIN fields marked away from password-manager heuristics
4. Test a workaround pass aimed specifically at Bitwarden heuristics:
   * login fields: username/current-password only
   * signup fields: username/new-password/new-password
   * reset-PIN section: current-password for account verification, explicit opt-out markers for PIN-only fields if a safe standards-based option exists
5. Verify whether the console error still occurs after each markup change, not just whether autofill visually works.
6. If the error persists with standards-compliant markup, record it as an upstream Bitwarden defect and link the upstream report directly from this issue.
7. Add a short compatibility note to internal QA guidance so Bitwarden stays in the auth regression checklist.

## Acceptance criteria

* No Bitwarden overlay exception is triggered on the main login screen during normal page load and autofill interaction.
* Login autofill still works for email/password.
* Signup autofill does not misclassify confirm-password or PIN fields.
* Reset PIN flow does not prompt Bitwarden against PIN-only fields.
* If upstream-only, issue contains reproduction steps, our tested mitigations, and the upstream tracker link.

## Fallback workaround if upstream bug remains

* Keep the current standards-based auth markup cleanup.
* Add any additional low-risk field disambiguation that improves Bitwarden behavior without harming browser/native autofill.
* Document the remaining error as external and avoid risky app-side hacks tied to Bitwarden internals. title: Investigate Bitwarden autofill overlay error on auth fields
description:
## Summary

Bitwarden's browser autofill overlay throws a runtime error on our auth screen:

`bootstrap-autofill-overlay.js:9562 Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'includes')`

This still happens after we updated the login/signup/password-reset fields to use more standard form semantics (`form` submit handling, `name`, and `autocomplete` hints).

## Impact

* Users with the Bitwarden autofill extension see a console error on our auth experience.
* The page appears usable, but this is noisy and may indicate Bitwarden is failing to classify some fields correctly.
* We should aim for our auth UI to behave cleanly with Bitwarden because it is a common password manager.

## Error

`bootstrap-autofill-overlay.js:9562 Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'includes')`

Stack excerpt:

* `AutofillOverlayContentService.setQualifiedLoginFillType`
* `AutofillOverlayContentService.isIgnoredField`

## Current app-side mitigation already tried

* Added `onSubmit` form semantics for login.
* Added explicit field names and autocomplete hints:
  * login email: `autocomplete=username`
  * login password: `autocomplete=current-password`
  * signup password fields: `autocomplete=new-password`
  * reset-password verification field: `autocomplete=current-password`
  * PIN-only fields: `autocomplete=off`

Files touched:

* `src/App.jsx`
* `src/pages/tracker/SettingsPage.jsx`

## Investigation notes

* The stack trace points at Bitwarden-injected code, not our bundled app code.
* There are recent public reports that match this same Bitwarden overlay error, so this may be an upstream extension bug.
* We should still document any DOM patterns in our app that make Bitwarden more likely to mis-detect fields.

## Acceptance ideas

* Reproduce with Bitwarden enabled on the auth screen.
* Confirm whether any remaining field markup can be made more Bitwarden-friendly.
* If no additional local workaround exists, track upstream and document as external compatibility issue.

## Implementation plan

1. Reproduce consistently with Bitwarden enabled on the login, signup, and PIN reset flows in desktop Chrome and one Chromium-based mobile wrapper build if available.
2. Capture a minimal DOM snapshot of the auth fields at the moment Bitwarden injects its overlay so we can compare field names, ids, labels, autocomplete values, and form ancestry.
3. Normalize auth markup further if needed:
   * add stable input ids paired with explicit `label htmlFor` or equivalent accessible relationships
   * ensure each auth flow is wrapped in a distinct `form`
   * avoid ambiguous password-like fields in the same visual section when possible
   * keep non-account PIN fields marked away from password-manager heuristics
4. Test a workaround pass aimed specifically at Bitwarden heuristics:
   * login fields: username/current-password only
   * signup fields: username/new-password/new-password
   * reset-PIN section: current-password for account verification, explicit opt-out markers for PIN-only fields if a safe standards-based option exists
5. Verify whether the console error still occurs after each markup change, not just whether autofill visually works.
6. If the error persists with standards-compliant markup, record it as an upstream Bitwarden defect and link the upstream report directly from this issue.
7. Add a short compatibility note to internal QA guidance so Bitwarden stays in the auth regression checklist.

## Acceptance criteria

* No Bitwarden overlay exception is triggered on the main login screen during normal page load and autofill interaction.
* Login autofill still works for email/password.
* Signup autofill does not misclassify confirm-password or PIN fields.
* Reset PIN flow does not prompt Bitwarden against PIN-only fields.
* If upstream-only, issue contains reproduction steps, our tested mitigations, and the upstream tracker link.

## Fallback workaround if upstream bug remains

* Keep the current standards-based auth markup cleanup.
* Add any additional low-risk field disambiguation that improves Bitwarden behavior without harming browser/native autofill.
* Document the remaining error as external and avoid risky app-side hacks tied to Bitwarden internals.

title: Make responsive layout handling reactive across tracker and outsider screens
description: Audit the app's mobile and desktop breakpoints and stop deriving responsive state directly from window.innerWidth during render. Several layouts and pages compute isMobile or viewport-dependent UI without reactive resize handling, which can leave the interface in the wrong mode after resize, rotation, or viewport changes. This should be fixed with a shared responsive strategy so tracker and outsider shells stay consistent across device changes.

title: Break up App.jsx and reduce shell-level complexity
description:
Refactor the app architecture to reduce delivery risk from oversized top-level files and tightly coupled state. src/App.jsx has grown into a very large controller that mixes auth, profile loading, tracker state, outsider state, connection workflows, support inbox handling, event normalization, preview data, and tutorial logic. Split the app into clearer modules or hooks so future feature work is easier to reason about, test, and ship safely.

Implementation plan

1. Identify clean module boundaries such as auth/profile, tracker data, outsider data, connections/support, calendar normalization, and preview-only logic.
2. Extract read-only helpers first to reduce risk before moving stateful flows.
3. Move coherent state and side effects into focused hooks or controller modules with stable inputs and outputs.
4. Reduce the size of App.jsx incrementally while keeping behavior and prop contracts stable.
5. Add or strengthen tests around any extracted logic that is easy to regress.
6. Stop once the top-level app shell reads as orchestration rather than implementation.

Definition of done

* App.jsx is materially smaller and easier to scan.
* Major data domains are separated into clearer modules or hooks.
* Behavior remains unchanged aside from structural cleanup.

title: Clean up mobile UI across tracker and outsider flows
description:

This issue should cover responsive cleanup across both tracker and outsider flows, especially key daily pages, calendar surfaces, support screens, and navigation patterns, so the app feels intentional and usable on phone-sized screens instead of compressed or cluttered.

Implementation plan

1. Audit the worst mobile screens first: tracker overview, tracker log flows, calendar, outsider overview, outsider telemetry, and support pages.
2. Capture the biggest phone-sized pain points in spacing, overflow, crowded navigation, and visual hierarchy.
3. Fix layout and spacing issues in the shared shells before polishing individual pages.
4. Tidy page-level grids, cards, buttons, and section framing so content reads clearly on narrow widths.
5. Verify the bottom nav, headers, and action areas remain reachable without overlapping key content.
6. Re-test on multiple mobile viewport sizes and keep screenshots or notes for before-and-after validation.

Definition of done

* Core tracker and outsider pages feel intentional on phone-sized screens.
* Major overflow, crowding, and awkward spacing problems are resolved.
* Navigation remains usable without hiding important content.
